title: RA.985 Lechuga Zafiro

translation.content: Percussion is, at the root, a conversation. It's about different instruments meeting each other, and interacting to form something bigger than the sum of its parts. Few engage in this dialogue as boldly as Pablo de Vargas, AKA Uruguayan experimentalist Lechuga Zafiro, who draws from tradition to make sounds like candombe and clave feel, well, completely new. Uruguay, the second smallest country in South America, has long punched above its weight in electronic music. It's the birthplace of underground and largely stripped-back luminaries like Nicolas Lutz, DJ Koolt and Z@p. But de Vargas follows a different trajectory. His music reaches outward, building bridges between Montevideo and Bogotá, Tijuana, Berlin and beyond. He's a key figure in the hybridisation of Latin American club music, with albums on labels like NAAFI and TraTraTrax. His RA Podcast plays like a manifesto in motion. RA.985 opens with a recording of Jorginho Gularte, an Uruguayan composer, playing a jazz rhythm. From there, it expands: there's the candombe-inflected pulse of a Gábor Lázár rework, the club chaos of DJ Fucci and Syntrovert, the percussion-heavy futurism of Doctor Jeep and DJ Babatr, and moments of pure groove from Leonce, DJ Nervoso and Ehua. Cuban guaguancó, Venezuelan drums, batida, tribal, techno—it's all here, stitched together with precision and intention. de Vargas is reckoning with these roots. His 2018 EP Testigo confronted the colonial violence embedded in the history of the Río de la Plata. His sets are similarly alive with memory, asking, without nostalgia: what does it mean to inherit rhythm? Who gets to carry it forward? He's also just a killer DJ, one of those rare artists who uses CDJs like an instrument. His sets are full of hot cues, delay FX and left turns. It's technical, but never cold. It's, in a word, funky. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I recently moved to Barcelona. Been low-key studying salsa percussion here, focusing on timbales. I have also been playing my live set at cute festivals like CTM and Rewire. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix covers everything I love about dance music: sabor, rawness, trance-inducing repetition, South American rhythms. It was recorded with three CDJs 3000 and a DJM900nxs2 at a studio in Barcelona. This set up is crucial for my DJing style: I use CDJ's hot cues and DJM's different delay FXs to rhythmically displace the tracks and create permutations that add a sensation of movement (and it’s also fun). Regarding the tracks, as synthetic or acoustic they might feel, they’re all related to the Clave. Clave is a five stroke motif that originated in West Africa and has been pivotal in our South American musical identity. I'm obsessed with how there's a whole set of rules around it. I've been trying to apply this language into the way I play. It ends with a recording of my music idols, Cuareim 1080, an Uruguayan candombe comparsa. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Finishing a club EP using some field recordings I've done recently, and working on a live/hybrid show with my friend Verraco. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I've honestly never been so pessimistic about the world situation. This reactionary, unpunished and global fascism rise is really grim. And it worries me how it's all connected: from the US to Israel to Argentina to Europe. It feels as if we're inevitably entering this neo-medieval dark phase. I put the emphasis on inevitable because I guess history sometimes shows us that these waves can feel unstoppable, we just need to navigate through them by trying to find common sense between our peers and keep on fighting for diversity and what we believe in through music. The illegal deporting despite Court orders in the US, the overall surreal insanity of the Argentinian government, the rhetoric of the Israeli government and genocide of the Palestinian people—even though really different in severity and context, it all feels part of the same irrational madness. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I can't just name one. Hiedrah from Buenos Aires has been crucial in my first years as a DJ, a place where I had the freedom to be as experimental as I wanted. Last year I played Mamba Negra in São Paulo for the first time and that was also a life changer.
translation.blurb: Relentless rhythms and Latin dance history, courtesy of the TraTraTrax innovator.
